title: Activation of X-Succinate Synthases for Fumarate Hydroalkylation Using an In Vitro Activation Method
author: Anshika Vats
license_note: PMC full-text XML — Bio-protocol — CC-BY confirmed in article XML

Reagents for radical installation and quantification
Reagents for hydroalkylation reactions and analysis

or buffers (Recipes 13–15); however, if used for glycerol stocks of strains, autoclave on standard liquid cycle (25 min sterilization time).

steps.title: was cloned into pET28a(+) (Novagen, kanamycin...
steps.instruction: was cloned into pET28a(+) (Novagen, kanamycin resistant) at restriction sites BamHI and HindIII. The N-terminal His-tag was removed, and a C-terminal His-tag was added. The full sequence can be found in Figure S

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Both N-terminal and C-terminal His-tags produced soluble IbsAE; however, slightly higher yields were obtained when the tag was at the C-terminus.

steps.title: pET-DUET-BSSαγ
steps.instruction: pET-DUET-BSSαγ. The gene for BSSα (Uniprot ID O68395 ) was cloned into the multiple cloning site 2 (MCS2), and BSSγ (Uniprot ID- O68394 ) was cloned into the multiple cloning site 1 (MCS

steps.title: of pET-DUET-1 (Novagen, ampicillin resistant)
steps.instruction: of pET-DUET-1 (Novagen, ampicillin resistant). A C-terminal His-tag was added to BSSα. BSSγ remained untagged. The full sequence can be found in Figure S

steps.title: pRSF-DUET-BSSβ
steps.instruction: pRSF-DUET-BSSβ. The gene for BSSβ (Uniprot ID O68396 ) was cloned into the MCS2 of pRSF-DUET-1 (Novagen, kanamycin resistant). An N-terminal His-tag was added to BSSβ. The full sequence can be found in Figure S

steps.instruction: Stain for SDS-PAGE (LC6065 SimplyBlue Safe Stain, catalog number:

steps.title: LB media (1 L) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: LB media (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount LB n/a 20 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 L Total 1 L After mixing LB and water, autoclave on a standard liquid cycle (25 min sterilization time). Note: 1 L of media is typically made in a 2.8 L flask, capped with aluminum foil, and then autoclaved. Alternatively, media can be made in autoclavable bottles and autoclaved. In this case, empty flasks should be capped with aluminum foil and autoclaved as well.

steps.title: LB/agar media (25 mL = 1...
steps.instruction: LB/agar media (25 mL = 1 LB/agar plate) Reagent Final concentration Amount Agar 1.5% (w/v) 375 mg LB media n/a To 25 mL Antibiotics Varies with antibiotic 25 μL a. After mixing LB and agar, autoclave on standard liquid cycle (25 min sterilization time). Note that agar will not dissolve until autoclaved. b. Gently swirl to thoroughly mix. Cool solution to ~50 °C and add appropriate antibiotic. Final concentration (ampicillin = 100 μg/mL; kanamycin = 50 μg/mL). c. Gently mix the antibiotic into the solution and pour into the plate. Note: This recipe makes one agar plate; however, more can be made by scaling up the recipe.

steps.title: 50% glycerol stock (1 L) Reagent...
steps.instruction: 50% glycerol stock (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount Glycerol 50% (v/v) 50 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 50 mL Total 100 mL 50% glycerol stock is used for making TB, buffers, and glycerol stocks of strains. The stock does not need to be autoclaved if used in TB (Recipe

steps.title: TB media (1 L) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: TB media (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount TB n/a 47.6 g 50% glycerol stock n/a 8 mL Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 L Total 1 L After mixing TB, glycerol, and water, autoclave on a standard liquid cycle (25 min sterilization time). Note: 1 L of media is typically made in a 2.8 L flask, capped with aluminum foil, and then autoclaved. Alternatively, media can be made in autoclavable bottles and autoclaved. In this case, empty flasks should be capped with aluminum foil and autoclaved as well before use.

steps.title: Kanamycin stock (10 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Kanamycin stock (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount Kanamycin sulfate 1,000× (50 mg/mL) 500 mg Milli-Q Water n/a To 10 mL Total 10 mL Aliquot as desired and store at -20 °C.

steps.title: Ampicillin stock (10 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Ampicillin stock (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount Ampicillin 1,000× (100 mg/mL) 1 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 10 mL Total 10 mL Aliquot as desired and store at -20 °C.

steps.title: Iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate stock...
steps.instruction: Iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate stock (1 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount Iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate 1,000× (75 mg/mL) 75 mg Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. Dissolve iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate in water (vortexing the solution can speed this up). b. Pass stock through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to sterilize before use.

steps.title: L-cysteine hydrochloride stock (1 mL) Reagent...
steps.instruction: L-cysteine hydrochloride stock (1 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount L-cysteine hydrochloride 1,000× (23.5 mg/mL) 23.5 mg Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. Dissolve L-cysteine hydrochloride in water (vortexing the solution can speed this up). b. Pass stock through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to sterilize before use.

steps.title: 1 M IPTG stock (10 mL)...
steps.instruction: 1 M IPTG stock (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount IPTG 1 M 2.383 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 10 mL Total 10 mL Aliquot as desired and store at -20 °C.

steps.title: 1 M HEPES stock, pH 8
steps.instruction: 1 M HEPES stock, pH 8.0 (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount HEPES 1 M 238.3 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 L Total 1 L Adjust the pH to 8.0 by adding NaOH and store at 4 °C. Note: Dissolve HEPES in approximately 800 mL water, adjust the pH, then add water to reach a final volume of 1 L. The recipe can be scaled down as desired.

steps.title: 5 M NaCl stock (1 L)...
steps.instruction: 5 M NaCl stock (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount NaCl 5 M 292.2 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 L Total 1 L Note: The recipe can be scaled down as desired.

steps.title: 2 M imidazole stock, pH 8
steps.instruction: 2 M imidazole stock, pH 8.0 (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount Imidazole 2 M 136.2 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 1 L Total 1 L Adjust the pH to 8.0 by adding HCl and store at 4 °C. Note: Dissolve imidazole in approximately 800 mL of water, adjust the pH, then add water to reach a final volume of 1 L. The recipe can be scaled down as desired.

steps.title: Lysis/equilibration buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Lysis/equilibration buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 1 M HEPES stock 50 mM 25 mL 5 M NaCl stock 300 mM 30 mL 50% glycerol stock 5% 50 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 395 mL Total 500 mL a. Store in a cold room until use. b. Sparge on a Schlenk line for 20 min with nitrogen gas before bringing into the anaerobic chamber. Note: This recipe is specifically for use with TALON resin. If using Ni Sepharose resin, imidazole should be added to a final concentration of 10 mM.

steps.title: Wash buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Wash buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 1 M HEPES stock 50 mM 25 mL 5 M NaCl stock 300 mM 30 mL 2 M imidazole stock 5 mM* 1.25 mL 50% glycerol stock 5% 50 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 393.75 mL Total 500 mL a. Store in a cold room until use. b. Sparge on a Schlenk line for 20 min with nitrogen gas before bringing into the anaerobic chamber. *Note: This recipe is specifically for use with TALON resin. If using Ni Sepharose resin, the final concentration of imidazole should be 20 mM.

steps.title: Elution buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Elution buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 1 M HEPES stock 50 mM 25 mL 5 M NaCl stock 300 mM 30 mL 2 M imidazole stock 200 mM* 50 mL 50% glycerol stock 5% 50 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 345 mL Total 500 mL a. Store in a cold room until use. b. Sparge on a Schlenk line for 20 min with nitrogen gas before bringing into the anaerobic chamber. *Note: This recipe is specifically for use with TALON resin. If using Ni Sepharose resin, the final concentration of imidazole should be 300 mM.

steps.title: Desalting/activation buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: Desalting/activation buffer (500 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 1 M HEPES stock 50 mM 25 mL 5 M NaCl stock 300 mM 30 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 445 mL Total 500 mL a. Store in a cold room until use. b. Sparge on a Schlenk line for 20 min with nitrogen gas before bringing into the anaerobic chamber.

steps.title: 1× SDS-PAGE running buffer (1 L)...
steps.instruction: 1× SDS-PAGE running buffer (1 L) Reagent Final concentration Amount 10× Tris/Glycine/SDS buffer 1× 100 mL Milli-Q Water n/a 900 mL Total 1 L

steps.title: 10 mM 5-deazariboflavin stock (2
steps.instruction: 10 mM 5-deazariboflavin stock (2.66 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 5-deazariboflavin 10 mM 10 mg DMSO n/a To 2.66 mL Total 2.66 mL a. 5-deazariboflavin is light sensitive. Store in 100 μL aliquots in black Eppendorf tubes at -20 °C after freezing in liquid nitrogen. Alternatively, clear tubes can be wrapped in aluminum foil. b. Transfer an aliquot to the anaerobic chamber in cold beads (-20 °C) prior to activation reactions.

steps.title: 100 mM dithiothreitol stock (DTT) (1...
steps.instruction: 100 mM dithiothreitol stock (DTT) (1 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount Dithiothreitol 100 mM 15.4 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. For desalting/activation buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Store in 100 μL aliquots in Eppendorf tubes at -20 °C after freezing in liquid nitrogen. c. Transfer an aliquot to the anaerobic chamber in cold beads (-20 °C) prior to activation reactions.

steps.title: 36 mM AdoMet stock (1 mL)...
steps.instruction: 36 mM AdoMet stock (1 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount AdoMet 36 mM 27.6 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. For desalting/activation buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Adjust pH with 10 M NaOH until the solution reaches neutral pH as indicated by pH strips. c. Store in 100 μL aliquots in Eppendorf tubes at -20 °C after freezing in liquid nitrogen. d. Transfer an aliquot to the anaerobic chamber in cold beads (-20 °C) prior to activation reactions.

steps.title: Fremy’s salt stock Reagent Final concentration...
steps.instruction: Fremy’s salt stock Reagent Final concentration Amount Fremy’s salt X mM ~3–8 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. For desalting/activation buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Cycle a bottle of Fremy’s salt into the anaerobic chamber. c. Add Fremy’s salt to an Eppendorf tube and add activation buffer. The solution should appear pale purple when near the desired final concentration range (~1–3 mM). d. Transfer a small amount of this solution (20–50 μL) to a clean Eppendorf tube and bring this aliquot out of the anaerobic chamber to find the radical concentration by UV-Vis spectroscopy. e. Measure the absorbance of the Fremy’s aliquot at 248 nm. The molar absorptivity is 1,690 M -1 ·cm -1 . Use the Beer-Lambert law ( A 248 = ε 248 bC , where A 248 is absorbance at 248 nm, ε 248 is molar absorptivity at 248 nm, b is the length of the light path, and C is the concentration of the solution) to find the final concentration of Fremy’s radical. f. The anaerobic Fremy’s stock can then be used to make dilutions for EPR analysis. Notes:

steps.title: Fremy’s stock is prepared fresh each...
steps.instruction: Fremy’s stock is prepared fresh each time a standard curve is made for radical quantification.

steps.title: While buffer conditions used to dissolve...
steps.instruction: While buffer conditions used to dissolve Fremy’s salt can vary, buffers with DTT or reductant are not compatible as they quench the radical.

steps.title: 100 mM fumarate stock (1 mL)...
steps.instruction: 100 mM fumarate stock (1 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount Sodium fumarate dibasic 100 mM 16.0 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 1 mL Total 1 mL a. For desalting/activation buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Store in 100 μL aliquots in Eppendorf tubes at -20 °C after freezing in liquid nitrogen. c. Transfer an aliquot to the anaerobic chamber in cold beads (-20 °C) prior to activation reactions.

steps.title: 2 mM 3-chlorobenzoic acid stock (10...
steps.instruction: 2 mM 3-chlorobenzoic acid stock (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 3-chlorobenzoic acid 2 mM 3.1 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 10 mL Total 10 mL a. For desalting/activation buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: 20 mM 2-benzylsuccinic acid stock (10...
steps.instruction: 20 mM 2-benzylsuccinic acid stock (10 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 2-benzylsuccinic acid 20 mM 41.6 mg Desalting/activation buffer n/a To 10 mL Total 10 mL a. For desalting/activation Buffer, see Recipe

steps.title: SDS-PAGE loading dye (20 mL) Reagent...
steps.instruction: SDS-PAGE loading dye (20 mL) Reagent Final concentration Amount 2-Mercaptoethanol 5% (v/v) 500 μL Bromophenol blue 0.02% (w/v) 4 mg 50% glycerol 30% (v/v) 12 mL SDS 10% (w/v) 2 g Tris-Cl 250 mM 0.788 g Milli-Q Water n/a To 20 mL Total n/a 20 mL Laboratory supplies

steps.title: Seals for LCMS (BioChromato, catalog number
steps.instruction: Seals for LCMS (BioChromato, catalog number: R80.120.

steps.title: Select the appropriate expression media based on the strain
steps.instruction: Select the appropriate expression media based on the strain: a. For expression of IbsAE, use TB media (see Recipe

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. For expression of BSSβ or BSSαγ, use LB media (see Recipe

steps.title: Add the correct antibiotic to agar...
steps.instruction: Add the correct antibiotic to agar plates and liquid cultures according to the strain: a. For IbsAE or BSSβ, supplement with 100 μL of kanamycin stock (see Recipe

steps.title: to achieve a final concentration of 50 μg/mL
steps.instruction: to achieve a final concentration of 50 μg/mL. b. For BSSαγ, supplement with 100 μL of ampicillin stock (see Recipe

steps.title: Purifications can be performed using either...
steps.instruction: Purifications can be performed using either TALON (Co 2+ ) or Ni Sepharose (Ni 2+ ) resins. While this protocol is designed for TALON, Ni Sepharose is a viable alternative. Key differences include higher (imidazole) in buffers (see Recipes 13–

steps.title: when using Ni Sepharose and higher...
steps.instruction: when using Ni Sepharose and higher loading capacity with Ni Sepharose. Purification on Ni Sepharose often results in slightly higher impurity levels. A

steps.title: Thaw 100 μL of chemically competent E
steps.instruction: Thaw 100 μL of chemically competent E. coli NiCo21(DE

steps.title: Mix cells gently with 10–50 ng of plasmid DNA (e
steps.instruction: Mix cells gently with 10–50 ng of plasmid DNA (e.g., pET-DUET-BSSαγ, pRSF-DUET- BSSβ, pET-28a-IbsAE) using a pipette. Avoid rough mixing.

steps.title: Incubate plasmid–cell mixture on ice for...
steps.instruction: Incubate plasmid–cell mixture on ice for 30 min to facilitate plasmid adhesion to cell membranes.

steps.title: Heat-shock cells at 42 °C for...
steps.instruction: Heat-shock cells at 42 °C for 45 s in a water bath to induce transient membrane permeability.

steps.title: Immediately return to ice for 4...
steps.instruction: Immediately return to ice for 4 min to stabilize the cells.

steps.title: Add 500 μL of SOC outgrowth...
steps.instruction: Add 500 μL of SOC outgrowth media and recover at 37 °C for 1 h with shaking (220 rpm).

steps.title: Plate 100 μL of the recovery...
steps.instruction: Plate 100 μL of the recovery culture onto LB agar plates supplemented with desired antibiotics.

steps.title: Spread evenly using a sterile glass...
steps.instruction: Spread evenly using a sterile glass spreader, then incubate plates upside down at 37 °C for 16–18 h.

steps.title: After 16–18 h, inspect plates for isolated, single colonies
steps.instruction: After 16–18 h, inspect plates for isolated, single colonies. An isolated, single colony will be picked for inoculation of the starter culture (see below). Note: Agar plates can be stored at 4 °C for up to 2 weeks. A

steps.title: Pour 100 mL of LB media...
steps.instruction: Pour 100 mL of LB media into a 500 mL Erlenmeyer flask. Note: The starter culture will be used to inoculate expression cultures at a 1:10 starter-to-expression ratio, meaning 10 mL of starter culture should be used per 1 L of expression culture. The volume of starter culture can be adjusted accordingly.

steps.title: Add 100 μL of the desired antibiotic to the media
steps.instruction: Add 100 μL of the desired antibiotic to the media.

steps.title: Inoculate the starter culture with a...
steps.instruction: Inoculate the starter culture with a single colony from the transformation plate (step 9 from A

steps.title: Incubate the starter culture overnight at...
steps.instruction: Incubate the starter culture overnight at 37 °C at 220 rpm for 12–16 h ( Figure 2A ). Figure

steps.title: Protocol for bacterial growth and protein production
steps.instruction: Protocol for bacterial growth and protein production. (A) Starter cultures are prepared by inoculation using a single colony from the transformation plate into LB media and are incubated overnight (12–16 h) to establish initial bacterial growth. (B) Expression cultures are inoculated with 10 mL of starter culture into fresh growth medium supplemented with iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate, L-cysteine, and the appropriate antibiotic, followed by incubation at 37 °C at 220 rpm. (C) Bacterial growth in expression cultures is monitored by measuring OD 600 . Once the OD 600 reaches 0.6–0.8, expression cultures should be cooled at 4 °C for 30 min, after which IPTG is added to induce protein production. (D) Post-induction incubation of the expression culture requires reduced temperature (22 °C) and slower agitation (100 rpm) for proper protein folding and [4Fe–4S] incorporation. A

steps.title: Expression cultures
steps.instruction: Expression cultures

steps.title: In a 2
steps.instruction: In a 2.8 L Fernbach flask, add 1 mL of antibiotic stock solution to 1 L of pre-sterilized media (LB or TB).

steps.title: Supplement the media with 1 mL...
steps.instruction: Supplement the media with 1 mL of iron (II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate stock and 1 mL of L-cysteine hydrochloride stock.

steps.title: Inoculate the prepared sterile media with...
steps.instruction: Inoculate the prepared sterile media with 10 mL of starter culture ( Figure 2B ).

steps.title: Incubate cultures at 37 °C at...
steps.instruction: Incubate cultures at 37 °C at 220 rpm until the OD 600 = 0.6–0.8 (~2.5–4 h). Note: To measure OD 600 , use a sterile pipette tip to transfer 0.75–1 mL of bacterial culture to a clean cuvette. Measure the optical density at a 600 nm wavelength using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer. A higher OD 600 corresponds to higher cell density.

steps.title: Transfer the cultures from 37 °C...
steps.instruction: Transfer the cultures from 37 °C to 4 °C for 30 min. Note: Cultures can be placed in a cold room set to 4 °C or placed on an ice bath. Higher protein yields are obtained when this cooling step is incorporated into the expression procedure.

steps.title: All four genes in this study...
steps.instruction: All four genes in this study (genes encoding BSSα, BSSγ, BSSβ, and IbsAE) are under the control of the T7 promoter. To induce gene expression, add 100 μL of IPTG stock for a final concentration of 100 μM ( Figure 2C ).

steps.title: Reduce temperature to 22 °C and...
steps.instruction: Reduce temperature to 22 °C and shaking speed to 100 rpm. Incubate for 12–16 h ( Figures 2D and 3A ). Note: Reduced shaking incorporates less oxygen into cell cultures, which is beneficial for [4Fe–4S] cluster incorporation into the proteins. Figure

steps.title: Protocol for anaerobic protein lysis and purification
steps.instruction: Protocol for anaerobic protein lysis and purification. (A) Expression culture prior to cell harvesting. (B) Harvested bacterial cell pellet after centrifugation, followed by immediate flash-freezing in liquid nitrogen and storage at -80 °C. (C) Cell lysis via sonication performed within an anaerobic chamber, with cell lysate embedded in cold beads to mitigate heat generation. (D) Clarified lysate after high-speed centrifugation (conducted outside the anaerobic chamber). (E) TALON resin prior to protein binding, displaying its characteristic pink color. (F) Protein-bound TALON resin exhibits a brownish color, indicating successful binding of the [4Fe–4S] cluster-containing protein to the resin. (G) Final purified protein sample after desalting and concentration, exhibiting the distinctive brown color typical of intact [4Fe–4S] clusters.

steps.title: Transfer expression cultures to centrifuge bottles
steps.instruction: Transfer expression cultures to centrifuge bottles.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 2,700× g at 4...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 2,700× g at 4 °C for 30 min to pellet cells.

steps.title: Decant and discard supernatant
steps.instruction: Decant and discard supernatant.

steps.title: Resuspend the cell pellet in 30...
steps.instruction: Resuspend the cell pellet in 30 mL of lysis/equilibration buffer and transfer to a 50 mL conical tube. Note: We typically transfer cells from 2 L of expression culture into a single conical tube. The lysis and purification protocols were developed accordingly. If only 1 L of expression culture is used, the protocols for lysis and purification can be scaled down.

steps.title: Centrifuge again at 2,700× g at...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge again at 2,700× g at 4 °C for 20 min. Discard supernatant.

steps.title: Flash-freeze pellets by submerging Falcon tubes...
steps.instruction: Flash-freeze pellets by submerging Falcon tubes in liquid nitrogen ( Figure 3B ).

steps.title: Store at -80 °C until the purification step
steps.instruction: Store at -80 °C until the purification step. A

steps.title: Anaerobic cell lysis Critical
steps.instruction: Anaerobic cell lysis Critical: Conduct lysis and purification in an MBraun anaerobic chamber (N 2 atmosphere). Alternatively, use a Coy anaerobic chamber validated for equivalent performance (97.5% N 2 , 2.5% H 2 atmosphere). Critical: All the buffers used in lysis and purification are sparged with nitrogen (or argon) gas and subsequently cycled into the chamber. Keep buffers at 4 °C throughout lysis and purification. This can be done in an anaerobic chamber by keeping buffers in cold beads.

steps.title: Cycle the cell pellets inside the...
steps.instruction: Cycle the cell pellets inside the anaerobic chamber while still frozen and let them thaw for 30–60 min. Note: Conical tubes must be cracked open during cycling of the antechamber.

steps.title: Weigh out approximately 40 mg of...
steps.instruction: Weigh out approximately 40 mg of lysozyme into a serum bottle or equivalent container. Cover in parafilm. Puncture 2–3 small holes in the parafilm and cycle the bottle into the anaerobic chamber.

steps.title: In the anaerobic chamber, add 40...
steps.instruction: In the anaerobic chamber, add 40 mL of lysis/equilibration buffer to the lysozyme (final concentration of ~1 mg/mL) and gently mix. Add the resulting lysozyme solution to the cell pellet. Note: Adding more lysozyme is not problematic.

steps.title: Supplement with 25 μL of DNase I (2
steps.instruction: Supplement with 25 μL of DNase I (2.5 MU/mL) and resuspend the cell pellet. Note: Using a spatula to mash the cell pellet can accelerate the resuspension step. Optional: The buffer can also be supplemented with an EDTA-free protease inhibitor cocktail to mitigate proteolytic degradation. Alternatively, phenyl methyl sulfonyl fluoride (PMSF) can be added as a serine protease inhibitor.

steps.title: Cycle cold beads (at -20 °C)...
steps.instruction: Cycle cold beads (at -20 °C) into the antechamber immediately before sonication.

steps.title: Sonicate resuspended cells using the following parameters
steps.instruction: Sonicate resuspended cells using the following parameters: 2 cycles of 2 min each, pulse: 2 s on, 2 s off, amplitude:

steps.title: Maintain samples in cold beads during...
steps.instruction: Maintain samples in cold beads during sonication to prevent protein denaturation ( Figure 3C ).

steps.title: Cold beads should be put at...
steps.instruction: Cold beads should be put at -20 °C the day prior to lysis to thoroughly cool them.

steps.title: Cells can be sonicated directly in...
steps.instruction: Cells can be sonicated directly in the conical tube or transferred to a metal beaker for sonication.

steps.title: Following cellular disruption by sonication, cycle...
steps.instruction: Following cellular disruption by sonication, cycle the lysate out of the anaerobic chamber in a sealed tube for clarification (i.e., removal of insoluble cellular debris). In the centrifuge, spin at 14,000× g for 45 min at 4 °C. Note: Tubes for clarification should have an O-ring in the cap to seal when brought out of the anaerobic chamber. Oxygen will eventually permeate these tubes; therefore, there should be no delay between steps 7 and

steps.title: Carefully aspirate the clarified supernatant, transferring...
steps.instruction: Carefully aspirate the clarified supernatant, transferring it to clean Falcon tubes. Exercise caution to avoid the pelleted debris and lipid layer, as these will interfere with subsequent chromatographic steps. Clarified lysate should appear brown due to the [4Fe–4S] clusters, although this color will vary based on protein ( Figure 3D ). Note: If desired, lysate can be filtered through a 0.22 or 0.45 μm syringe filter. A

steps.title: Pack 1 mL (settled volume) of...
steps.instruction: Pack 1 mL (settled volume) of TALON ® resin into a gravity-flow column (e.g., Pierce TM centrifuge columns) ( Figure 3E ). Note: Ni Sepharose Excel resin can also be used. Imidazole concentrations in buffers will vary, and the binding capacity of resin will vary. We recommend using less volume of resin if using Ni Sepharose Excel resin.

steps.title: Equilibrate the resin with 15–20 column...
steps.instruction: Equilibrate the resin with 15–20 column volumes (CV) of lysis/equilibration buffer. Allow the buffer to flow by gravity. Optional: Buffers can also be passed through the resin by centrifugation. Low speeds (100× g ) and short times (15–30 s) are required so that resin does not dry out.

steps.title: Apply the clarified lysate (from step 9 of A
steps.instruction: Apply the clarified lysate (from step 9 of A

steps.title: to the equilibrated resin
steps.instruction: to the equilibrated resin. Collect the flowthrough into clean 50 mL conical tubes for subsequent analysis by SDS-PAGE. Notes:

steps.title: Monitor resin color
steps.instruction: Monitor resin color. Unbound cobalt resin appears pink ( Figure 3E ); successful binding of Fe–S cluster-containing IbsAE, BSSαγ, and BSSβ induces a visible shift to dark brown upon passing all the clarified lysate through the resin ( Figure 3F ).

steps.title: If the lysate has not been...
steps.instruction: If the lysate has not been properly clarified, this step will be very slow by gravity.

steps.title: Wash the resin with 20 CV...
steps.instruction: Wash the resin with 20 CV of wash buffer to remove weakly bound contaminants. Collect the wash after passing through the resin into clean 50 mL conical tubes for subsequent SDS-PAGE analysis.

steps.title: Apply 500 μL of elution buffer
steps.instruction: Apply 500 μL of elution buffer. Discard this initial volume.

steps.title: Immediately after the 500 μL of...
steps.instruction: Immediately after the 500 μL of elution buffer has settled into the resin, place a clean conical under the column, and elute bound protein with 2.5 mL elution buffer. Collect the entire fraction, which will appear brown due to the [4Fe–4S] cluster. A

steps.title: Secure a PD-10 desalting column vertically in a Falcon tube
steps.instruction: Secure a PD-10 desalting column vertically in a Falcon tube. Wrap the column with parafilm to prevent premature buffer flow during cycling into the anaerobic chamber. Cycle into the anaerobic chamber.

steps.title: Equilibrate the column with 25 CV...
steps.instruction: Equilibrate the column with 25 CV (25 mL) of desalting/activation buffer. Allow the buffer to drain completely.

steps.title: Slowly load the 2
steps.instruction: Slowly load the 2.5 mL immobilized metal affinity chromatography (IMAC) eluate onto the PD-10 resin bed. Allow the IMAC eluate to drain completely.

steps.title: Place the desalting column into a...
steps.instruction: Place the desalting column into a clean conical tube for protein collection. Add 3.5 mL of desalting/activation buffer to the column. Collect the eluate as a single 3.5 mL fraction.

steps.title: Transfer to a centrifugal filter
steps.instruction: Transfer to a centrifugal filter. Centrifuge at 4,000× g in 5 min intervals at 4 °C until the retentate volume reaches the desired volume (typically between 250 and 500 μL). Notes:

steps.title: Concentrate proteins using the appropriate centrifugal...
steps.instruction: Concentrate proteins using the appropriate centrifugal filters (50 kDa for BSSαγ and 10 kDa for IbsAE/BSSβ) inside the anaerobic chamber.

steps.title: Convenient working concentrations range from 200...
steps.instruction: Convenient working concentrations range from 200 to 375 μM for IbsAE, from 150 to 300 μM for BSSαγ, and from 400 to 800 μM for BSSβ. Over-concentration can cause protein precipitation.

steps.title: Transfer concentrated protein into an Eppendorf tube and thoroughly mix
steps.instruction: Transfer concentrated protein into an Eppendorf tube and thoroughly mix. Save a small sample for SDS-PAGE analysis and for protein concentration determination. Dispense the remaining protein into 50–100 μL aliquots to minimize freeze-thaw cycles when using protein for reactions.

steps.title: Cycle aliquots out of the anaerobic...
steps.instruction: Cycle aliquots out of the anaerobic chamber and immediately immerse in liquid nitrogen.

steps.title: Store at -80 °C
steps.instruction: Store at -80 °C. A

steps.title: Cycle fractions of flowthrough from IMAC...
steps.instruction: Cycle fractions of flowthrough from IMAC (10 μL), wash from IMAC (10 μL), and final concentrated protein (1 μL) out of the box. Dilute concentrated protein with 9 μL of Milli-Q water.

steps.title: Add 5 μL of SDS-PAGE loading...
steps.instruction: Add 5 μL of SDS-PAGE loading dye to all fractions and mix gently.

steps.title: Heat samples for 2 min at...
steps.instruction: Heat samples for 2 min at 95 °C prior to electrophoresis to denature protein.

steps.title: Insert gel (4%–20%) into the electrophoresis...
steps.instruction: Insert gel (4%–20%) into the electrophoresis gel holder and insert the gel holder into the tank.

steps.title: Add 1× running buffer inside the...
steps.instruction: Add 1× running buffer inside the electrophoresis gel holder to fully cover gel wells.

steps.title: Load 2 μL of protein ladder...
steps.instruction: Load 2 μL of protein ladder and 6 μL of each sample in gel wells.

steps.title: Connect the tank to the power...
steps.instruction: Connect the tank to the power supply and run gel at 270 V for 25 min.

steps.title: Remove gel from plastic shell and...
steps.instruction: Remove gel from plastic shell and place in a Tupperware container with dye. Microwave for 1 min and place on an orbital shaker for 10 min to stain the gel.

steps.title: Destain the gel by replacing dye...
steps.instruction: Destain the gel by replacing dye with Milli-Q water and shaking until gel is destained (~30 min).

steps.title: Identify protein bands based on molecular...
steps.instruction: Identify protein bands based on molecular weight ( Figure 4 ) (99.0 and 6.9 kDa for purified BSSαγ, 40.3 kDa for purified IbsAE, and 11.7 kDa for purified BSSβ). Figure

steps.title: SDS-PAGE analysis of all protein components used in this study
steps.instruction: SDS-PAGE analysis of all protein components used in this study. (A) Lane

steps.title: IbsAE (40
steps.instruction: IbsAE (40.3 kDa). Stars denote proteins of interest. A

steps.title: Cycle an aliquot of desalted, concentrated...
steps.instruction: Cycle an aliquot of desalted, concentrated protein from step 6 of A6 out of the box for protein concentration determination via UV/Vis spectrophotometry.

steps.title: Dispense 4 μL of purified protein...
steps.instruction: Dispense 4 μL of purified protein sample into each of three wells. Load 4 μL of Milli-Q water into three separate wells as blanks.

steps.title: Measure absorbance at 280 nm (A...
steps.instruction: Measure absorbance at 280 nm (A 280 ) using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer.

steps.title: Calculate the mean A 280 for...
steps.instruction: Calculate the mean A 280 for the sample and the blank.

steps.title: Subtract the mean blank A 280...
steps.instruction: Subtract the mean blank A 280 from the mean sample A 280 .

steps.title: Determine protein concentration using Beer-Lambert law
steps.instruction: Determine protein concentration using Beer-Lambert law: C = (A 280 / ε 280 ) * (1/ b ), where C = concentration (M), ε 280 = extinction coefficient (M -1 ·cm -1 ) at wavelength 280 nm, b = path length (cm) Notes:

steps.title: Extinction coefficients at wavelength 280 nm are as follows
steps.instruction: Extinction coefficients at wavelength 280 nm are as follows: IbsAE = 36,900 M -1 ·cm -1 ; BSSαγ = 141,150 M -1 ·cm -1 ; BSSβ = 17,990 M -1 ·cm -1 .

steps.title: Ensure the spectrophotometer is calibrated for microplate path length
steps.instruction: Ensure the spectrophotometer is calibrated for microplate path length. Adjust the concentration calculation if using a pre-programmed microplate protocol.

steps.title: Expected yields from purifications range from...
steps.instruction: Expected yields from purifications range from 6 to 8 mg/L for IbsAE, 6–10 mg/L for BSSαγ, and 5–7 mg/L for BSSβ. B. Glycyl radical installation and quantification B

steps.title: Enzyme activation (glycyl radical installation)
steps.instruction: Enzyme activation (glycyl radical installation): 300 μL activation reaction

steps.title: Example of calculations for activation reaction...
steps.instruction: Example of calculations for activation reaction (300 μL reaction) Reagents [Stock] [Final] Volume Desalting/activation buffer n/a n/a 110.7 μL DTT 100 mM 1 mM 3 μL 5-deazariboflavin 10 mM 50 μM 6 μL IbsAE 197 μM 50 μM 76.2 μL BSSαγ 164 μM 50 μM 91.6 μL AdoMet 36 mM 1.5 mM 12.5 μL

steps.title: Add desalting/activation buffer to a 1
steps.instruction: Add desalting/activation buffer to a 1.7 mL Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Add DTT (final concentration of 1...
steps.instruction: Add DTT (final concentration of 1 mM) and 5-deazariboflavin (final concentration of 50 μM). Mix gently.

steps.title: Add freshly thawed IbsAE to the...
steps.instruction: Add freshly thawed IbsAE to the reaction mixture (final concentration of 50 μM) and mix gently with a pipette.

steps.title: Add freshly thawed BSSαγ to the...
steps.instruction: Add freshly thawed BSSαγ to the reaction mixture (final concentration of 50 μM) and mix gently with a pipette.

steps.title: Initiate activation by adding AdoMet (final concentration of 1
steps.instruction: Initiate activation by adding AdoMet (final concentration of 1.5 mM).

steps.title: Incubate the reaction in a metal...
steps.instruction: Incubate the reaction in a metal heat block at 25 °C for 3–24 h. Notes:

steps.title: Transfer IbsAE and BSSαγ into the...
steps.instruction: Transfer IbsAE and BSSαγ into the anaerobic chamber right before the reaction.

steps.title: Reducing the temperature significantly slows the...
steps.instruction: Reducing the temperature significantly slows the rate of activation, while higher temperatures can cause the proteins to precipitate.

steps.title: Reactions require exposure to light, and...
steps.instruction: Reactions require exposure to light, and the fluorescent front-mounted lamp provided with the MBraun anaerobic chamber was found to be sufficient for this purpose, even when tubes were in the heat block. Too much light has been found to cause irreproducibility and lower levels of glycyl radical installation. When reactions are conducted within a Coy chamber, ambient light from the surrounding lab environment is sufficient.

steps.title: Higher concentrations of 5-deazariboflavin (>200 μM)...
steps.instruction: Higher concentrations of 5-deazariboflavin (>200 μM) can also cause irreproducibility and lower levels of glycyl radical installation.

steps.title: The activation reaction size can range...
steps.instruction: The activation reaction size can range from 25 μL to 2 mL. Critical step: Mix reactions gently by pipetting up and down three times without introducing any air. B

steps.title: SDS-PAGE to monitor activation SDS-PAGE can...
steps.instruction: SDS-PAGE to monitor activation SDS-PAGE can be used as a method to analyze activation reactions. When the glycyl radical of BSSα is exposed to molecular oxygen, BSSα is cleaved into two polypeptide chains at the site of the radical (93.3 and 5.7 kDa). The amount of unactivated BSSα in a reaction (99.0 kDa) can be compared to activated BSSα (93.3 kDa) by SDS-PAGE ( Figure 5 ) [19]. Although this method is useful for monitoring activation reactions and qualitatively assessing reaction progress, there are limitations to this method as a quantitative tool. Instead of directly measuring radical, here we are observing protein cleavage as a result of the radical. For the direct quantification of radical, see section B

steps.title: Exposure of glycyl radical to oxygen leads to protein cleavage
steps.instruction: Exposure of glycyl radical to oxygen leads to protein cleavage. SDS-PAGE analysis of BSSα cleavage. Lanes 1–

steps.title: Unactivated BSSα as a control (99
steps.instruction: Unactivated BSSα as a control (99.0 kDa). Lanes 4–

steps.title: Activated BSSα exposed to oxygen, showing...
steps.instruction: Activated BSSα exposed to oxygen, showing a double band corresponding to cleaved N-terminal fragment (93.3 kDa) and residual unactivated BSSα (99.0 kDa).

steps.title: Cycle 1 μL of activation reaction...
steps.instruction: Cycle 1 μL of activation reaction and 1 μL of unactivated BSSαγ out of the anaerobic chamber and expose to air. Note: For the unactivated BSSαγ control, it is recommended to use an activation reaction with a key reagent left out (e.g., AdoMet).

steps.title: Add 24 μL of Milli-Q water...
steps.instruction: Add 24 μL of Milli-Q water to both tubes containing activation reaction and unactivated BSSαγ.

steps.title: Pipette 5 μL of each diluted...
steps.instruction: Pipette 5 μL of each diluted sample from the previous step into new Eppendorf tubes.

steps.title: Add 5 μL of SDS-PAGE loading...
steps.instruction: Add 5 μL of SDS-PAGE loading dye to both tubes and mix gently.

steps.title: Heat samples for 2 min at...
steps.instruction: Heat samples for 2 min at 95 °C prior to electrophoresis to denature protein.

steps.title: Insert gel (7
steps.instruction: Insert gel (7.5%) into the electrophoresis gel holder and insert the gel holder into the tank.

steps.title: Add 1× running buffer inside of...
steps.instruction: Add 1× running buffer inside of the electrophoresis gel holder to fully cover gel wells.

steps.title: Load 2 μL of protein ladder...
steps.instruction: Load 2 μL of protein ladder and 4 μL of each sample into gel wells.

steps.title: Connect the tank to a power...
steps.instruction: Connect the tank to a power supply and run gel at 270 V for 25 min.

steps.title: Remove gel from plastic shell and...
steps.instruction: Remove gel from plastic shell and place in a Tupperware container with dye. Microwave for 1 min and place on an orbital shaker for 10 min to stain the gel.

steps.title: Destain the gel by replacing dye...
steps.instruction: Destain the gel by replacing dye with Milli-Q water and shaking until gel is destained (~30 min).

steps.title: A single band at 99
steps.instruction: A single band at 99.0 kDa for control should be observed for unactivated BSSαγ. A double band at 99.0 and 93.3 kDa should be observed for activated BSSαγ. The ratio of the 93.3 kDa band to the 99.0 kDa band provides an indication of the extent of activation. B

steps.title: EPR spectroscopy to quantitate glycyl radical...
steps.instruction: EPR spectroscopy to quantitate glycyl radical As described previously for many other GREs [8,10,20], the glycyl radical can be directly observed using EPR spectroscopy ( Figure 6A ). Here, we describe how to determine the concentration of glycyl radical within BSSα using Fremy’s salt as a standard. Figure

steps.title: The glycyl radical in BSSα can...
steps.instruction: The glycyl radical in BSSα can be observed and quantitated using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. (A) Representative EPR spectrum of activated BSSα, showing the characteristic doublet. (B) Known concentrations of Fremy’s radical can be plotted vs. the double integral of the resulting EPR signal to produce a standard curve for quantification. The curve can be used, along with the double integral of the unknown sample's signal, to determine the radical concentration of the unknown (e.g., glycyl radical).

steps.title: Set up activation reaction according to section B1 above
steps.instruction: Set up activation reaction according to section B1 above. Typically, 250–300 μL reactions are recommended for the most accurate quantification. Note: Radical signal is dependent on the length and position of the sample in the resonator. By increasing reaction volume to 250–300 μL, the length of the sample will be longer than the resonator and remove the sample-to-sample discrepancies that could arise from shorter sample lengths.

steps.title: At the time of quantification, prepare...
steps.instruction: At the time of quantification, prepare a Fremy’s stock solution (Recipe

steps.title: in the anaerobic chamber
steps.instruction: in the anaerobic chamber. Record the concentration of the stock. Use this stock to make a series of dilutions using desalting/activation buffer. The recommended range for these standards is 5–50 μM final concentration of Fremy’s salt.

steps.title: Cycle in EPR tubes, glass transfer...
steps.instruction: Cycle in EPR tubes, glass transfer pipettes, pipette bulb, and liquid nitrogen into the anaerobic chamber.

steps.title: Transfer 250–300 μL of activation reaction...
steps.instruction: Transfer 250–300 μL of activation reaction and Fremy’s standards into EPR tubes using a glass transfer pipette.

steps.title: Slowly plunge the filled EPR tubes...
steps.instruction: Slowly plunge the filled EPR tubes in liquid nitrogen to freeze. Note: Rapid freezing could break the tubes.

steps.title: Bring the samples out of the...
steps.instruction: Bring the samples out of the anaerobic chamber on liquid nitrogen.

steps.title: Collect EPR spectra for activation and Fremy’s standards
steps.instruction: Collect EPR spectra for activation and Fremy’s standards. Representative parameters: temperature = 120 K (note that temperatures from 77 to 120 K can be used for glycyl radical quantification with minimal change in signal), center field = 3320 G, sweep width = 200 G, sweep time = 60 s, receiver gain = 60 dB, modulation amplitude = 3 G, microwave power = 1.26 μW, time constant = 20.48 ms, conv. time = 89.96 ms. Notes:

steps.title: It is highly recommended to optimize conditions
steps.instruction: It is highly recommended to optimize conditions. When optimizing conditions, collect spectra of samples at two microwave power settings that differ by a factor of four (e.g., 1.26 μW and 0.315 μW). The spectrum collected at lower power (e.g., 0.315 μW) should have a two-fold lower signal intensity. Use Xenon software to multiply the lower power spectrum by a factor of two. Overlay the two spectra. If the two spectra do not perfectly overlay, the conditions are not suitable for quantification.

steps.title: Spectra for all samples must be...
steps.instruction: Spectra for all samples must be collected using the exact same parameters; any variation compromises quantification.

steps.title: Use Xenon software to find the...
steps.instruction: Use Xenon software to find the double integrals for spectra at optimized conditions for the sample and standards.

steps.title: Plot the known concentration of Fremy’s...
steps.instruction: Plot the known concentration of Fremy’s salt against the double integral to create a standard curve ( Figure 6B ).

steps.title: Use the standard curve and double...
steps.instruction: Use the standard curve and double integral of sample to calculate the concentration of glycyl radical. C. Using activated BSS for hydroalkylations C

steps.title: Example of calculations for hydroalkylation reaction...
steps.instruction: Example of calculations for hydroalkylation reaction (50 μL reaction) Reagents [Stock] [final] Volume Desalting/activation buffer n/a n/a 5.7 μL Fumarate 100 mM 2 mM 1 μL Toluene neat 1% (v/v) 0.5 μL BSSβ 723 μM 40 μM 2.8 μL Activated BSSαγ 50 μM 40 μM 40 μL

steps.title: Begin by pipetting desalting/activation buffer into an Eppendorf tube
steps.instruction: Begin by pipetting desalting/activation buffer into an Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: Add fumarate and toluene
steps.instruction: Add fumarate and toluene. Mix gently.

steps.title: Add BSSβ to the reaction mixture and mix gently
steps.instruction: Add BSSβ to the reaction mixture and mix gently.

steps.title: Initiate the reaction by adding 40...
steps.instruction: Initiate the reaction by adding 40 μL of activation reaction to achieve a final concentration of 40 μM BSSαγ and mix gently.

steps.title: Perform the reaction at room temperature...
steps.instruction: Perform the reaction at room temperature and incubate on a heat block at 25 °C for approximately 1 h to achieve 89%–97% assay yield ( Figure 7 ). Figure

steps.title: After approximately 1 h of incubation,...
steps.instruction: After approximately 1 h of incubation, remove the reaction from the anaerobic chamber and quench with 50 μL of methanol. Notes:

steps.title: Transfer BSSβ into the anaerobic chamber right before the reaction
steps.instruction: Transfer BSSβ into the anaerobic chamber right before the reaction.

steps.title: Assays are recommended to be performed in triplicate
steps.instruction: Assays are recommended to be performed in triplicate. Critical step: Mix reactions gently by pipetting up and down three times without introducing any air. C

steps.title: Add 2 mM 3-chlorobenzoic acid solution...
steps.instruction: Add 2 mM 3-chlorobenzoic acid solution as an internal standard (I.S.) to the quenched reaction in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio relative to the reaction volume.

steps.title: Centrifuge the reaction sample for 20...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the reaction sample for 20 min at maximum speed in a centrifuge to pellet denatured protein.

steps.title: Gently decant the supernatant using a...
steps.instruction: Gently decant the supernatant using a pipette and dilute it 20-fold with Milli-Q water.

steps.title: Filter the diluted solution through a 0
steps.instruction: Filter the diluted solution through a 0.22 μm filter for LC–MS analysis. A syringe filter is sufficient if a small number of samples is being analyzed; however, a 96-well plate filter plate can be used for larger numbers of samples. Note: 96-well filter plates can be reused. We recommend passing Milli-Q water through them between each use.

steps.title: Calculate the enzymatic product concentration using...
steps.instruction: Calculate the enzymatic product concentration using the ratio of the area under the peaks corresponding to the [M-H] ion of benzylsuccinic acid (m/z = 207.

steps.title: Determine the product concentration using a...
steps.instruction: Determine the product concentration using a standard curve of 2-benzylsuccinic acid. Define the assay yield as [product] / [limiting reagent] × 100%. C

steps.title: Starting with 20 mM benzylsuccinic acid...
steps.instruction: Starting with 20 mM benzylsuccinic acid (Recipe 24), perform serial dilutions in Milli-Q water to generate the following concentrations: 2.5 mM, 1250 μM, 625 μM, 312.5 μM, 156.25 μM, 78.13 μM, 39.06 μM, 19.53 μM, 9.77 μM, and 4.88 μM.

steps.title: Pipette 50 μL of each standard...
steps.instruction: Pipette 50 μL of each standard solution into separate wells of a 96-well plate.

steps.title: in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio relative...
steps.instruction: in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio relative to the standard volume and mix gently.

steps.title: Add 50 μL of methanol to...
steps.instruction: Add 50 μL of methanol to each mixture (to match hydroalkylation reaction conditions) and mix gently.

steps.title: Dilute the mixture 20-fold with Milli-Q...
steps.instruction: Dilute the mixture 20-fold with Milli-Q water and filter through a 0.22 μm syringe filter or centrifuge using a 96-well plate filter.

steps.title: To generate a standard curve, plot...
steps.instruction: To generate a standard curve, plot the ratio of the LC–MS peak area for the product ion to the peak area of the 3-chlorobenzoic acid ion ( Figure 7C ) against the known benzylsuccinic acid concentrations.

steps.title: Use linear regression to generate the calibration curve
steps.instruction: Use linear regression to generate the calibration curve. C

steps.title: LC–MS/MS method for assessing enzyme assays...
steps.instruction: LC–MS/MS method for assessing enzyme assays LC–MS data were obtained using a Waters Acquity Premier UPLC–MS system. For ionization, the mass spectrometer is equipped with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source. Separation of the samples was carried out on a reversed-phase CORTECS Premier C18+ 1.6 μm 2.1 mm × 50 mm column, using Milli-Q water with 0.1% formic acid as solvent A and acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid as solvent B. The injection volume was 10 μL, flow rate was set to 0.8 mL/min, and gradient elution was performed as follows: t = 0 min, 5% B; t = 1.5 min, 95% B; t = 2.3 min, 95% B; t = 2.5 min 5% B; t = 3.0 min 5% B. MS detection was performed via ESI source in negative ionization mode. Product formation was monitored in selective ion recording (LC–MS-SIR) mode. Detection targeted two ions: benzylsuccinate ([M−H] = 207.1 m/z) and the internal standard 3-chlorobenzoic acid ([M−H] = 155.1 m/z). The ratios of peak areas were compared to the standard curve and used to quantify product yield.

description: Validation of protocol This protocol has been used and validated in the following research article: • Andorfer et al. [1]. Development of an in vitro method for activation of X-succinate synthases for fumarate hydroalkylation. iScience. For activations, n = 3. For hydroalkylations, n = 9. These protocols were developed in Prof. Cathy Drennan’s lab at MIT [1]. All protocols herein were reproduced in Prof. Mary Andorfer’s lab at MSU (this work).
